claims.claim_text: A named November 2018 holiday-search rollout at a leading global fashion retailer is presented as a real incident.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: In November 2018, a leading global fashion retailer deployed a highly anticipated feature for the holiday shopping season: an updated search engine for their catalog.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A retail search rollout can fail when a catalog search system relies too heavily on exact keyword matching.
claims.rationale: The module gives a dated, real-world-style anecdote without a named company or attributable primary source.

claims.claim_text: The retailer's null-result rate spiked by twenty-two percent within hours.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Internal monitoring dashboards triggered critical alerts as the "null search result" rate spiked by twenty-two percent globally within a matter of hours.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Monitoring often shows a sharp rise in null-result rates when a keyword-only search rollout misses the language users actually type.
claims.rationale: This is a precise operational metric attached to an unattributed incident.

claims.claim_text: The retailer lost twelve million dollars over a three-day holiday weekend because of the rollout.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Over a grueling three-day holiday weekend period, the retailer estimated a direct, unrecoverable revenue loss of twelve million dollars.
claims.suggested_rewrite: During a peak sales window, a failed search rollout can translate into substantial lost revenue.
claims.rationale: This is fake-precise loss accounting for an unattributed anecdote.

claims.claim_text: Production embeddings often have exactly 384, 768, or 1536 dimensions.
claims.span_hint: section: Visualizing Embeddings in 2D and 3D Space, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: Real-world production embeddings often contain 384, 768, or even up to 1536 distinct spatial dimensions.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Real-world production embeddings often contain hundreds or even thousands of dimensions.
claims.rationale: The exact dimension list spans multiple model families and would need multiple precise sources to support honestly.

claims.claim_text: HNSW is the undisputed industry standard for vector search speed/recall trade-offs.
claims.span_hint: section: Building Production Semantic Search, paragraph after naive_search example
claims.anchor_text: The Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) algorithm is currently the undisputed industry standard for balancing extreme retrieval speed and high operational recall within vector topology.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) algorithm is a widely used ANN approach that often offers a strong speed-recall trade-off for vector search.
claims.rationale: The superlative "undisputed industry standard" is not supportable from a single authoritative source.

claims.claim_text: Qdrant is open source, has a cloud offering, and is best for general purpose use and Rust performance.
claims.span_hint: section: Building Production Semantic Search, database comparison table, Qdrant row
claims.rationale: This is a vendor comparison claim that points to an off-allowlist primary source.

claims.claim_text: Weaviate is open source, has a cloud offering, and is best for GraphQL API and multi-modal workloads.
claims.span_hint: section: Building Production Semantic Search, database comparison table, Weaviate row
claims.rationale: This is a vendor comparison claim that relies on an off-allowlist vendor source.

claims.claim_text: Milvus is open source, has a cloud offering, and is best for billion-scale vector workloads.
claims.span_hint: section: Building Production Semantic Search, database comparison table, Milvus row
claims.rationale: This is a vendor comparison claim with its primary product source outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Pinecone is not open source, has a cloud offering, and is best for managed ease of use.
claims.span_hint: section: Building Production Semantic Search, database comparison table, Pinecone row
claims.rationale: This is a vendor comparison claim that would need Pinecone's off-allowlist primary docs or product pages.

claims.claim_text: Chroma is open source, has no cloud offering, and is best for lightweight embeddings use cases.
claims.span_hint: section: Building Production Semantic Search, database comparison table, Chroma row
claims.rationale: This is a vendor comparison claim that depends on off-allowlist primary product documentation.

claims.claim_text: A brute-force search over one million documents takes 1,000ms and costs $0 in hardware.
claims.span_hint: section: The Surprising Economics of Vector Search, table row 1
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Brute Force (1M docs) | 1M | Hardware-dependent and usually much slower than ANN | No separate ANN index cost |
claims.rationale: These latency and cost numbers are presented as hard facts without any benchmark setup, hardware assumptions, or source.

claims.claim_text: FAISS HNSW over one million documents takes 1ms and costs $0 in hardware.
claims.span_hint: section: The Surprising Economics of Vector Search, table row 2
claims.suggested_rewrite: | FAISS HNSW (1M docs) | 1M | Often far lower latency than brute force | Extra memory and tuning overhead |
claims.rationale: The row gives exact benchmark outcomes and zero-cost implications without a reproducible source.

claims.claim_text: A brute-force search over one billion documents takes 1,000,000ms and costs $0 in hardware.
claims.span_hint: section: The Surprising Economics of Vector Search, table row 3
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Brute Force (1B docs) | 1B | Typically impractical without heavy optimization | No separate ANN index cost |
claims.rationale: This exact latency figure is unsupported and highly hardware-dependent.

claims.claim_text: FAISS HNSW over one billion documents takes 10ms and costs about $50K/year.
claims.span_hint: section: The Surprising Economics of Vector Search, table row 4
claims.suggested_rewrite: | ANN Index (1B docs) | 1B | Can be practical with specialized infrastructure | Cost depends heavily on hardware, replication, and recall targets |
claims.rationale: The exact latency and annual cost figures are unsourced pricing/benchmark claims.

claims.claim_text: Vector database deployments should target Kubernetes v1.35 or higher to maintain compatibility and support.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, final table row
claims.anchor_text: | Deploying to end-of-life Kubernetes | Running vector databases on deprecated orchestrators risks severe stability failures and security flaws. | Ensure all cluster deployments explicitly target K8s version v1.35 or higher to maintain strict compatibility and support. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Deploying to unsupported Kubernetes releases | Running critical infrastructure on unsupported orchestrators increases operational risk. | Keep deployments on a currently supported Kubernetes release. |
claims.rationale: Kubernetes supports multiple current minor versions, and the module gives no authoritative basis for a hard v1.35+ floor specific to vector databases.

claims.claim_text: Tomas Mikolov and colleagues submitted the Word2Vec paper on January 16, 2013.
claims.rationale: The arXiv record directly lists the submission date and paper title.

claims.claim_text: Google Search integrated BERT in October 2019 and improved over 10 percent of all global searches.
claims.anchor_text: In October 2019, Google search integrated massive BERT embeddings into their primary algorithm, instantly improving query comprehension and semantic matching for over 10 percent of all global searches.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In 2019, Google began using BERT in Search to improve query understanding.
claims.rationale: The official Google announcement is off-allowlist and scoped the figure to one in ten U.S. English searches, not all global searches.

claims.claim_text: Pinecone announced a $750M valuation in April 2023.
claims.rationale: Pinecone's official newsroom page states the $100M round at a $750M valuation, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: An exact brute-force search over one billion 768-dimensional vectors requires about 3TB of memory bandwidth per query.
claims.anchor_text: An unoptimized exact brute force search over 1 billion 768-dimensional vectors requires the hardware to stream approximately 3 terabytes of physical memory bandwidth per single user query.
claims.suggested_rewrite: An exact brute-force search over a billion high-dimensional vectors demands enormous memory bandwidth per query.
claims.rationale: The exact 3TB figure depends on unstated assumptions such as data type, storage layout, and implementation details.

further_reading.title: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space
further_reading.why_relevant: Foundational word-embedding paper that underpins the module's discussion of vector-space semantics.

further_reading.title: Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for HNSW, the ANN indexing approach referenced in the production search sections.

further_reading.title: FAISS
further_reading.why_relevant: Practical upstream implementation of dense-vector similarity search and ANN indexing used throughout the module.

notes: Code fences, Mermaid diagrams, and knowledge-check items were excluded per task rules; seeded claims focus on prose, comparison tables, and the 'Did You Know' section.
